segments.s614-g113: This clause states a general observation. جهان (subject) + هم (emphatic/also) precede the negative-result verb نگردد (subjunctive/present stem here meaning “become”); ز introduces the source (devoid of). تهی is a predicative adjective meaning “devoid (of).” Word order is Subject–(adverb)–Verb–complement, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s614-g114: An address + rhetorical question. تو (you) is topicalized; چندین چنان modifies چرا (here = “why”), forming “why so many/so often.” رنج بر تن نهی is a verbal phrase meaning “you place suffering upon the body”; رنج (object) takes the prepositional phrase بر تن (upon the body). The final نهی is an archaic/personal verb form implying ‘you place/lay’ (here not matching the modern senses listed), completing the question.
segments.s614-g115: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning “when.” بنشست is the past tense verb “he sat (down).” The prepositional phrase بر تخت شاه (on the king’s throne) follows the verb, and از نخست (literally “from the first”) means “at first/initially,” placed at the end for emphasis — a common classical word order.
segments.s614-g116: This is the main clause: به پیمان (for a covenant/pact) is the object and نجست (here meaning “sought/searched for,” past tense) is the verb; جز از چنگ شیران is an exception phrase introduced by جز (“except”), with از marking source. چنگ شیران “the claws/grip of the lions/brave men” is figurative — he accepted no agreement except what came by force/strong men.
segments.s614-g117: A simple declarative clause: کنون (now) sets time; شهریاری (sovereignty/kingship) is the subject; به ایران indicates the domain (over Iran); تراست is a fused form meaning “it is yours” (trā + ast ⇒ ‘is yours’), a possessive predication common in classical Persian.
segments.s614-g118: This is a rhetorical question. به گور آمدی literally “you have come to the grave” (i.e., to death); جنگ شیران is the subject of the following predicate; چراست (= چرا است) asks “why is it?” — together: “why has/does the battle of the brave men bring you to the grave?” The clause is elliptical and rhetorical, typical of the poem’s compressed style.
segments.s614-g119: بدو گفت is a reporting formula: بدو (“to him”) plus گفت (here used as ‘said’) — classical Persian commonly places the indirect object before the reporting verb. شاه‌ای خردمند پیر is a vocative address—شاه‌ای (“O king”) followed by two epithets: خردمند (wise) and پیر (old/elder), placed after the head noun for honorific emphasis.
segments.s614-g120: This is an elliptical clause reporting the speaker’s grim forecast: به شبگیر فردا places the time (literally “at nightfall tomorrow”); the verbless nominal sequence من و گور و تیر lists coordinated items (I, the grave, and the arrow) and implies the copular/resultive relation (“it will be…” or “it will be me, the grave, and the arrow”). Such verbless lists are frequent in Persian poetry.
segments.s614-g153: بدانگه (بـ + دانگه) is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment/thereupon'; که introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that') that follows. اسکندر آمد is a simple past clause 'Alexander came' (آمد is past tense of آمدن). ز روم places the origin with the poetic preposition ز = 'from' + روم = Byzantium/Eastern Rome.
segments.s614-g154: به ایران is a directional phrase 'into/to Iran' (به here = 'towards/into'); و connects the next predicate. ویران شد is a stative/resultative past meaning 'was ruined/laid waste' (شد as 'became/was'); این مرز و بوم is the subject/thing affected: 'this border and homeland' (مرز و بوم = realm/land).
segments.s614-g155: کجا is used rhetorically/exclamatorily ('how' / 'where' in an exclamation) rather than a literal location. ناجوانمرد بود is 'he was dishonourable/cruel' (بود = past 'was'). و درشت links a second predicate describing his character: درشت = 'rough, coarse, fierce' (an attributive adjective).
segments.s614-g156: چو here means 'when' introducing the circumstance; the sequence that follows gives the violent deed. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/then' (archaic ordering in poetry). از marks the group affected 'of/from' (i.e. out of the number of rulers). شهریاران is the plural 'kings/rulers'. بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'he killed/slain' (here context requires 'killed'); the provided lemma senses list does not include 'kill', so use -1 for that sense.
segments.s614-g157: لب خسروان is a genitive construction 'the lip(s) of the kings' (possessed+noun). پر ز نفرین = 'full of curse' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = poetic 'from/of'). اوست is the copula 'is his' or 'it is of him' (او = 'he', ست = 'is'), so the clause says the kings' lips are filled with his curse.
segments.s614-g158: همه روی گیتی = 'the whole face/surface of the world' (همه = 'whole/entire' here; روی = 'surface/face'; گیتی = 'world'). پر از کین = 'full of hatred/vengeance' (پر از = 'full of'); اوست closes with the copula 'is his/it is of him.' The order is typical Persian: topical/possessed phrase then predicate.
segments.s614-g159: کجا is again rhetorical ('how/where') asking incredulously. بر فریدون کنند آفرین is a verb phrase 'they make/say "bravo" to Fereydun' (بر = 'to/on' marking the object of the acclamation; کنند is present/subjunctive/optative plural 'they do/say'—in poetry the present can express repeated or general action; آفرین is the noun/interjection 'praise/bravo').
segments.s614-g160: برویست is a compound verbal form meaning roughly 'it is upon him/them' or 'lies upon' (here introducing who bears the curse). نفرین = 'curse'. ز marks source/agent 'from' or 'by'. جویای کین = 'those seeking vengeance' or the phrase 'in search of vengeance' (جویای = 'seeking', modifying the notion of people or intent; کین = 'vengeance/hatred'). Together: 'a curse is upon (them) from those who seek vengeance.'
segments.s614-g185: برفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb (perfective) meaning 'they went/left'; the prefixed بر- is a poetic directional/emphatic element ('away'). بازارگانان شهر is the subject phrase 'the merchants of the city' (بازارگانان = merchants, شهر modifies it as a genitive/attributive). The verb precedes the subject here, a permissible poetic word order in classical Persian.
segments.s614-g186: This is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز (here 'from/of'), repeated for each source: 'ز جز و ز برقوه' = 'from (the) جز and from (the) برقوه'. مردم is the noun 'the people' and the numerically quantified phrase دو بهر means 'two apiece' or 'two for each' (بهر functioning as a distributive/’each’). There is no new finite verb; this whole phrase attaches to the previous verb (they departed / having taken), so the sense is 'they left, taking from X and Y two from the people each.'
segments.s614-g187: بیابان is the subject 'the desert/wilderness.' چو here introduces the clause ('when' / 'as') and pairs with شد 'became' (شد = past 'become'). بازار چین is a predicate-complement 'a China-like market / a Chinese market' (چین = China) and ز بار ('because of the loads') is a causal adjunct introduced by the poetic preposition ز. Word order is classical: subject + temporal/concessive 'چو' + predicate + verb + causal phrase.
segments.s614-g188: بران‌سو is an adverbial 'to that side / thither.' که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('which/where'), and بد لشکر شهریار is a noun phrase 'the king's bad/hostile army' with بد as an attributive adjective before لشکر and شهریار as the possessor. The clause is elliptical (typical in poetry): the relative clause explains the direction referred to by بران‌سو and attaches to the previous clause.
segments.s614-g65: همه زیردستان به پیش سپاه is a noun phrase + prepositional phrase describing who moved. همه modifies زیردستان (modification comes before the noun). به پیش سپاه literally 'to/in front of the host/army' — به + پیش gives a directional/locational phrase 'toward the presence of the army'.
segments.s614-g66: برفتند is a past plural verb (بر‑ + رفتند) meaning 'they went/advanced'. هرجای is a free-relative adverb 'wherever' and introduces the location for the following verb; کندند is the past plural of کنـدن (here 'to dig') and governs چاه as its object: 'they dug pits.' Word order is Verb (برفتند) then adverbial clause (هرجای کندند چاه) describing concurrent or successive action.
segments.s614-g67: بدان تا combines the particle بَ/بِ + دان to form an adverbial 'thereby/so that' or 'with that' and تا introduces purpose/result. نهند is a subjunctive/imperative 3rd‑plural form of نهادن 'to place/set' (here used to express purpose after تا). از بر چاه is a locative phrase 'over/on the pit' (از بر = upon/over + چاه). The verb pertains to placing something over the pits.
segments.s614-g68: کنند is another 3rd‑plural present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do' used with the preceding تا to state the intended action. از بر چرخ repeats the locative 'on/over the cover/wheel' (چرخ here functions as a noun 'cover/wheel'). چینی سطرخ is a noun phrase in which چینی qualifies سطرخ; poetically this reads as a colored/ornamental covering (literal lexical senses vary). The clause thus says they made/placed crimson (چینی سطرخ) coverings on the lids of the pits.
segments.s614-g69: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/after that'. لشکر اندر همی تاخت is a verbal clause where اندر همی (in/among + the emphatic/continuative همی) gives a sense of 'together/in one body', and تاخت is the past tense 'galloped/charged'. The verb follows the usual Classical Persian order (subject marked earlier; action expressed with a simple past).
segments.s614-g70: خود و ویژگان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the king himself and the nobles/distinguished ones' (خود is emphatic 'himself'). تا به introduces the extent/destination 'as far as / up to', and نخچیرگاه is the destination 'hunting‑ground'. Word order is typical: subject(s) then path/goal phrase.
segments.s614-g71: بیابان سراسر پر از گور دید is a simple verbal clause with the object/scene first: بیابان سراسر (the plain throughout) + پر از گور (full of graves) and then دید, the past verb 'he/one saw'. سراسر and پر act as modifiers before the noun-phrase and دید is the past-tense viewing verb placed after the phrase.
segments.s614-g72: همه بیشه از شیر پرشور دید parallels the previous clause: همه ('every/all') modifies بیشه ('thicket/grove'), از شیر 'of lions' is a genitive-like construction indicating what the thicket is full of, and پرشور ('full of ardor/frenzy') modifies شیر. دید again is the past 'saw'. Adjective-noun order (پرشور modifying شیر) follows Persian norms.
segments.s614-g41: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” با مهرگانی is an adverbial/occasional phrase ‘on/for Mehrgān’ (Mehrgān being the festival); بپوشیم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive (classical Persian form with the prefix ب-) meaning ‘we put on / we wear.’ خز is the noun ‘fur/velvet’ as the object of بپوشیم.
segments.s614-g42: به نخچیر is a purposive or directional phrase ‘for/unto the hunt (for prey).’ باید شدن is a periphrastic expression of necessity: باید (it is necessary / must) + شدن (here functioning as the infinitive/lexical verb ‘to go/become/set out’) → ‘must set out.’ سوی جز is a prepositional phrase ‘toward elsewhere / toward other/other places’ (جز used in the sense ‘other/aside’ in classical usage).
segments.s614-g43: بدان here is a locative/demonstrative compound (به + دان) meaning ‘to/at that’ or ‘in that’ and governs دشت ‘plain’ (بدان دشت = ‘in that plain’). نخچیر کاری: نخچیر (‘game, prey’) modifies کاری; کاری here is a noun ‘a deed/exploit’ (classical poetic usage), and کنیم is first-person-plural subjunctive ‘let us do / we will do’ — together ‘let us do a hunting deed on that plain.’
segments.s614-g44: که introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning ‘so that / in order that.’ اندر جهان = ‘in the world’ (اندر = in, جهان = world). یادگاری is a noun meaning ‘a lasting memorial/inscription/souvenir,’ and کنیم is again ‘we shall/may make’ (subjunctive) — overall: ‘so that we make a memorial in the world.’
segments.s614-g45: کنون = ‘now’ (adverb). گردن گور is a genitive phrase ‘the neck of the gūr’ — here گور is the wild ass/onager rather than ‘grave’ (classical poetic animal). گردد is the present/subjunctive of شدن ‘become,’ and ستبر is a predicate adjective ‘stout/thick’; word order places the verb before the predicate adjective in this classical style.
segments.s614-g46: This clause is coordinated: دل شیر نر گیرد and (understood) رنگ (بگیرد) ببر. دل شیر نر گیرد literally ‘the male lion’s heart takes/seizes’ — i.e. ‘the male lion takes heart / becomes brave’ (گیرد = takes/seizes). و links the next noun phrase; رنگ ببر ‘the tiger’s color’ is governed by the same verb by ellipsis (i.e. ‘and the tiger takes on color/hue’). Note ببر here is the noun ‘tiger,’ not the imperative.
segments.s614-g47: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing hunting animals and equipment: سگ و یوز (‘dogs and cheetahs’) followed by با چرغ (‘with a lamp/light’) and دو more birds joined by و: شاهین و باز (‘falcon and hawk’). The conjunctions simply enumerate the items that are governed by the negative obligation in the next segment.
segments.s614-g48: نباید is an impersonal modal negative ‘one must not / it ought not to.’ کشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive ‘to lead / to take along / to proceed with’ (here ‘to take along’), so نباید کشیدن = ‘one must not take (them).’ به راه دراز is the prepositional phrase ‘on/to a long road’ (به = toward/on, راه = road/way, دراز = long).
segments.s614-g57: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from'; هر گوشه‌ای is a noun phrase 'every corner' (هر + گوشه + enclitic ـای). لشکری جنگجوی is a noun phrase in which لشکری ('an army/troop') is followed by جنگجوی as a postpositive modifier (the ezāfe linking is omitted in poetic diction), yielding 'a troop of warriors.'
segments.s614-g58: سوی شاه ایران = 'toward the King of Iran' (سوی = toward, followed by the target). نهادند is the 3rd person plural past of نهادن 'to place/set' and here forms the light-verb of the idiom 'روی نهادن (به)' = 'to turn one's face toward'; روی (face) is the object of نهادن. The word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (prepositional phrase before verb).
segments.s614-g59: ازیشان (از + ایشان) = 'from them.' گزین کرد is a two-word verbal expression where گزین (choose) plus کرد (did/made) yields the past 'he chose.' گردنکشان is the noun phrase 'the proud-necked' or 'rebels' serving as the direct object of گزین کرد; poetic style omits an ezāfe.
segments.s614-g60: کسی کو is a relative phrase 'anyone who' (کسی = someone/anyone; کو is the archaic/poetic relative particle 'who'). ز نخچیر دارد نشان = 'has a mark from the spoil/booty' with ز = 'from,' نخچیر = 'spoil/booty,' دارد = present 'has' (classical Persian often uses present in such relative clauses), and نشان = 'mark/sign.' The verb دارد comes at the end of the relative clause as normal.
segments.s614-g61: بیاورد is a narrative/past 3rd-person form 'he brought' (classical poetic usage). لشکر is the direct object 'an army/host.' به دشت شکار is the locative phrase 'to/into the hunting plain' (به = to; دشت = plain; شکار = hunt, used attributively). The verb before its object is an inversion typical of poetic style.
segments.s614-g62: سواران شمشیر زن ده هزار = 'ten thousand sword‑wielding horsemen.' شمشیر زن is a compound: شمشیر 'sword' + زن (here from زدن 'to strike') = 'sword-striking' i.e. 'sword-wielding'; it post-modifies سواران. The numeral ده هزار follows the noun phrase, a permitted poetic ordering meaning 'ten thousand.'
segments.s614-g63: ببردند is the 3rd person plural past 'they took away/carried off.' خرگاه و پرده‌سرای are two coordinated objects ('the stable/donkey-stall and the curtained dwelling/pavilion') linked by و. In poetry such compound place-names are common and the verb governs both nouns.
segments.s614-g64: همان خیمه = 'that very tent' (همان adding emphasis). و آخر و چارپای continues the list: آخر here functions as 'the rest/other paraphernalia' or 'end/remaining things' and چارپای = 'beasts of burden' (pack-animals). The items are all objects of the preceding verb ببردند.
segments.s614-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کافور شد is a simple past-state clause 'camphor came/was' (شد here = 'became/appeared'). مشک معیوب گشت is a second clause in which مشک (subject) + معیوب (predicate adjective) + گشت (past 'became') means 'the musk became defective.' The two past verbs (شد, گشت) show successive/state changes.
segments.s614-g26: به here has an instrumental/adverbial force ('by/through'), so 'by the camphor'; بر تاج is the prepositional phrase 'upon the crown' (بر = 'on'). ناخوب گشت uses گشت again as 'became' with ناخوب as predicate adjective 'unbecoming/bad.' Word order is preposition + noun + predicate, typical of Classical Persian.
segments.s614-g27: همی + verb expresses ongoing or habitual action ('I keep/shall keep doing'). بزم و بازی کنم is first‑person present/subjunctive (کنم) used narratively for habitual/future action: 'I shall feast and play.' تا دو سال marks the duration 'for/until two years' (تا as temporal 'until/for').
segments.s614-g28: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' لختی here means 'a short while' (not nakedness); شکَست is used as a noun/nominalized idea 'the breaking/end' and اندر آید (اندر + آید) is a poetic periphrasis meaning 'comes in/arrives.' به یال is the locative 'to/at the mane.' The clause therefore reads 'when a short time is broken/ended, it comes to the mane.'
segments.s614-g29: شوم is a first‑person subjunctive/optative 'may I be / I will be' or narrative future. پیش یزدان = 'in the presence of God.' بپوشم is subjunctive/intentional 'I will put on/wear' (prefixed ب- forming the present/subjunctive). پلاس is the object 'a coarse cloak/blanket.' The two verbs express intention: 'I will go before God and clothe myself in a coarse cloak.'
segments.s614-g30: نباشم is negative subjunctive/optative 'may I not be' or 'let me not be.' ز introduces the cause/reason 'because of/from,' گفتار او = 'his words,' and ناسپاس is predicate adjective 'ungrateful.' The whole clause reads 'may I not be, on account of his words, ungrateful.'
segments.s614-g31: به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'in/with joy' (به used adverbially). بسی روز functions as a temporal object 'many/enough days' (بسی here 'much/many'); بگذاشتم is first‑person past 'I spent/passed' (literally 'I put/left' but used idiomatically for spending time). Together: 'I spent many days in joy.'
segments.s614-g32: ز indicates cause/agent 'from/because of.' بادی is 'a (gust of) wind' or metaphorically a capricious chance; که is the relative/conjunction 'which/that.' بد بهره here means 'an ill share/ill fortune' (بد = 'bad', بهره = 'share/lot'), and برداشتم is past first person 'I took/received/suffered.' So literally: 'from a wind which I took a bad share (from)' — i.e. 'I suffered an ill lot because of a wind/fickleness.'
segments.s614-g81: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic usage). این بیشه is the noun phrase 'this thicket/grove'. از + شیر expresses being 'devoid of' or 'empty of lions' (از introduces the thing removed). گردد is the archaic/poetic present/future-like form of 'become' (from گشتن), and تهی is the predicative adjective 'empty' — together 'when this thicket becomes empty of lions'.
segments.s614-g82: خدنگم را has the noun 'خدنگ' (dagger) with the enclitic possessive -م (my) plus the object marker را; here -م is the possessive suffix 'my' (not the copula). گور گردد رهی: گردد (become) takes گور 'grave' as the predicate, and رهی qualifies it 'on the road / of the road' — literally 'my dagger will become a grave (for me) on the road.'
segments.s614-g83: ببود is an archaic past copula meaning 'it was' (used to mark the time). آن شب 'that night' and بامداد پگاه 'at early dawn' form a temporal sequence — two time expressions joined by و. The phrase sets the time frame for the action that follows.
segments.s614-g84: سوی بیشه رفتند is 'went toward the thicket'; سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)', بیشه the place. رفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they went'. In poetry the verb can appear before the subject, so شاه و سپاه (the king and the army) follow the verb as the agents.
segments.s614-g85: هم‌انگاه بیرون خرامید شیر: هم‌انگاه = 'immediately / at the same moment'; بیرون = 'out, outside'; خرامید is the past tense of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance proudly'; شیر is the subject 'the lion'. The word order places the temporal and locative adverbials before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s614-g86: This segment strings participial/adjectival elements describing the lion: دلاور شده = 'having become brave/valiant' (شده = 'become'); خورده is the perfect participle 'having eaten'; از گور links the source 'from the grave'; سیر functions as 'sated'/'full' (a stative adjective). Together they describe the lion as newly valiant and sated after feeding 'from the grave' (literal or figurative).
segments.s614-g87: به یاران چنین گفت بهرام گرد: به یاران is the dative prepositional phrase 'to his companions'; چنین 'thus/so' introduces the reported speech; گفت is the past 'said'. In poetic order the speaker's name بهرام گرد follows the verb. (بهرام گرد is the proper name/epithet; گرد here is part of the name.)
segments.s614-g88: که introduces the content of what he said ('that' / 'saying that'). تیر و کمان دارم is straightforward present-tense 'I have arrows and a bow' (دارم = 'I have'). و دست برد: دست 'hand' + برد used as a noun meaning 'victory/that which wins' yields 'a hand that wins' or 'a victorious hand' — a compact way to claim martial prowess.
segments.s614-g17: بی‌آزاری is a noun/adjective used as a quality — 'harmlessness' or 'being harmless'; و simply links two qualities; راستی here means moral uprightness/honesty; بایدت is the modal 'bāyad' with the second‑person enclitic -ت, giving 'you must' or 'it is necessary for you'. The phrase literally states the qualities and then the obligation: 'Harmlessness and uprightness you must (have).'
segments.s614-g18: چو is used as the conditional 'if'; خواهی is the 2nd‑person present 'you want/wish'; que/که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'; این خورده is 'this (that) having been eaten' where خورده is a past participle modifying این; نگزایدت is the negative verb form with the enclitic -ت meaning 'will not harm you' or 'will not befall you'. The whole clause: 'if you want that this eaten thing will not harm you.'
segments.s614-g19: کنون = 'now'; سال من = 'my year' (i.e. my age); رفت is the past/simple verb 'has gone' used idiomatically with بر + numeral to indicate reaching an age; بر سی و هشت = 'to/onto thirty‑eight.' Word order is temporal adverb → possessee → verb → prepositional phrase indicating the reached number.
segments.s614-g20: بسی here functions as 'many'/'much' (classical sense); روز = 'days'; بر + شادمانی indicates the domain or occasion ('in/for rejoicing'); گذشت is the past verb 'passed.' Together: 'Many days of rejoicing passed.'
segments.s614-g21: چو = 'when'; سال جوان = 'the (one's) young year' (i.e. youth); بر کشد is a classical verbal phrase meaning 'comes up to / draws on' (کشد here used as the verb 'to draw/come up') and is followed by بر چهل = 'to forty.' The structure states a temporal condition: 'When youth reaches forty.'
segments.s614-g22: غم = 'sorrow/grief' is the subject; روز مرگ is a genitive phrase 'the day of death'; اندرآید is the present/simple verb 'enters / comes into' (from اندرآمدن); به دل = 'into the heart.' Word order is subject → modifier → verb → prepositional complement.
segments.s614-g23: چو = 'when'; یک موی = 'one hair' (a single hair); گردد is the verb 'becomes/turns' (from گشتن); به سر = 'on the head'; بر سپید is an inverted/poetic order meaning 'white' (i.e. 'turns white on the head'). The clause describes the physical sign (first white hair).
segments.s614-g24: بباید is the modal 'one must / it is necessary' (archaic/poetic form of باید); گسستن is the infinitive/noun 'to break / to separate' and here means 'to give up' or 'to sever'; ز = 'from' (poetic equivalent of از); شادی امید = 'hope of joy' or 'hopes for rejoicing.' Together: 'It is necessary to break/abandon hope of joy.'
segments.s614-g33: This is a prepositional/nominal list introduced by the temporal adverb 'کنون' (now). 'بر' is a poetic preposition here meaning 'for/on/regarding' and links the following nouns as the objects or beneficiaries (گل, نار, سیب, بهی). Word order is noun after preposition in a coordinate series connected by 'و'.
segments.s614-g34: 'ز' (poetic 'از') marks source/possession here ('of/from the golden goblet'). 'می‌جام زرین' is a genitive/compound noun phrase ('golden wine-goblet'); 'ندارم' is first-person singular present ('I do not have'). 'تهی' is a predicate adjective meaning 'empty' and complements ندارم (i.e. there is nothing left — it is empty). The order places the negated verb before the adjective complement, a common classical pattern.
segments.s614-g35: 'چو' here introduces a temporal clause 'when'; it governs the verb 'بینم' (1st person singular present/subjunctive form 'I see'). The noun phrase 'رخ سیب' is a genitive construction ('the face/cheek of the apple'), followed by the adjectival phrase 'بیجاده رنگ' — a poetic compound meaning 'of variegated/mottled color' that modifies رخ. Note the verb of the main clause is postponed to the next segment.
segments.s614-g36: This is the main clause: 'شود' is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن ('become'); the subject follows ('آسمان' — the sky). 'همچو' introduces a simile ('like/as'), followed by the simile-term 'پشت پلنگ' ('the back of a leopard'). Poetic word order places the verb before its subject and the simile after it.
segments.s614-g37: 'برومند' is an adjective meaning 'flourishing/prosperous' modifying the implied subject (the season/spring). 'بویا' functions as an adjectival/poetic descriptor ('fragrant') — note this is an older/poetic form — and 'بهاری' specifies 'of spring'. 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The sequence lists adjectival qualities before the copula, a typical poetic order.
segments.s614-g38: 'می‌سرخ' is a present-stem verb/adjectival participle ('turns/was blushing red'); combined with the following 'بود' it yields a past descriptive sense ('it was blushing/red'). 'چون' introduces a simile ('like/as'), and 'غمگساری' is a poetic noun ('mourning/one in mourning' or 'lamentation') serving as the term of comparison. The final 'بود' is the past copula applying to the simile clause.
segments.s614-g39: 'هوا' is the subject 'the air/atmosphere'. 'راست گردد' uses 'راست' ('right/proper') plus 'گردد' (3rd-person present/subjunctive of 'گشتن/گردیدن' meaning 'become'); together they mean 'becomes proper/settled'. The correlative negative 'نه ... و نه ...' ('neither ... nor ...') negates both 'گرم' (warm) and 'سرد' (cold). The clause therefore describes a balanced temperate air.
segments.s614-g40: This is a verbless nominal-description: 'زمین سبزه' ('the earth [is] green') and 'آبها لاژورد' ('the waters [are] lapis/azure'). The copula 'است/بود' is omitted (understood) — a common poetic device — so the two noun–adjective pairs state the scene's colors.
segments.s614-g73: چنین گفت is a short clause meaning "he said thus/so": چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb, and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. کاینجا شکار منست follows as a nominal predicate: کاینجا = که + اینجا (a fused relative/locative 'that here' = 'here'), شکار 'prey/quarry', and منست is an archaic/copular phrase (من + است) meaning 'is mine' or 'belongs to me' (here functioning as the predicate). Word order is Topic — Verb then predicate clause.
segments.s614-g74: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). The phrase از شیر بر خاک is a set of prepositional/locative elements: از 'from/of' + شیر 'lion' + بر 'on/upon' + خاک 'ground/earth'. چندین تنست is the predicate: چندین 'several/many a' modifies تن ('bodies/persons') and تنست is an older concatenated form (تن + است) meaning 'there are bodies' or 'bodies exist'. The clause gives the reason why the place is the speaker's quarry.
segments.s614-g75: بخسپید is an older/imperfectly spelled imperative or jussive form meaning 'lie down/rest' (addressing plural or polite plural). It is followed by coordinated adjectival phrases: شادان‌دل 'cheerful-hearted' and تن‌درست 'sound/whole of body'. The conjunction و links the two adjectives describing the audience of the command.
segments.s614-g76: که begins an explanatory clause 'for/that'. فردا is temporal 'tomorrow'. بباید is a modal/auxiliary indicating necessity or inevitability (here rendering a future/necessary event). م + را is the split of the object 'مرا' (me + object marker را): the clitic m = 'me' and را marks the definite object. شیر is the subject 'the lion' and جست ('to leap') is the verb; together بباید ... جست expresses that 'tomorrow the lion will/must spring at me.'
segments.s614-g77: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. می‌گساریم is a first-person plural present/imperfect form with the prefix می‑ and ending ‑یم, here meaning 'we scatter / we disperse / we make an outing' (an ongoing or hortative action). تا is the temporal conjunction 'until', and چاک روز is a poetic phrase for 'the splitting of the day' = 'daybreak' (چاک here is figurative; the literal sense 'split/cleft' underlies the image).
segments.s614-g78: چو 'when' introduces a temporal subordinate clause. رخشان (radiant) is an adjective; شود is the present/future copular/auxiliary 'becomes / appears'. هور گیتی فروز is a compact poetic noun phrase: هور 'splendor/light' modified by گیتی‌فروز 'world-illuminating' (فروز here carrying the sense 'to illuminate' or 'brighten'), so the clause means 'when the radiant splendor that lights the world appears/becomes bright.'
segments.s614-g79: نخستین 'first' is an adverbial/ordering adverb. به + شمشیر is the instrumental phrase 'with the sword'. شیر is the direct object 'the lion'. افگنیم is an archaic first-person plural verb form (here future/intentional) meaning 'we will strike down / slay'. The verb is older/poetic and carries the main action applying to both coordinated objects in the next segment as well.
segments.s614-g80: همان 'that same' or 'likewise' points to a second target. اژدهای is 'dragon' (with ezāfe-like connection to the following adjective), دلیر 'brave' modifies it, and the verb افگنیم repeats (understood as applying to this object as well). The construction lists two victims/targets of the same act (first the lion, then that brave dragon).
segments.s614-g9: به introduces a destination/goal (here 'into/to'); دخمه درون is a prepositional noun phrase 'the inner tomb/vault'. بس is a verblike predicate 'enough' (poetic: 'it suffices'), and که introduces the following clause. تنهاشویم is a subjunctive/optative first-person-plural form ('that we be/let us be alone'), so the whole segment means 'it suffices to go into the inner vault, that we be alone.'
segments.s614-g10: اگر چند is a concessive phrase here ('if/though, even if'), with چند functioning as an archaic adverb meaning 'though/even if'. با introduces accompaniment or means. برز و بالا are paired epithets 'high and lofty' (adjectival nouns placed in coordination), and شویم is the present-subjunctive/optative 'we become/ascend'. Together: 'even if we rise to/are with high and lofty places.'
segments.s614-g11: همه ('all') is a quantifier; بسترد is an archaic/perfect past verb form (from سترد) meaning 'covered/overwhelmed' and here takes مرگ as the grammatical subject (poetic word order: verb before subject). دیوانها ('the divans/courts/offices') is the plural object; together: 'Death overwhelmed/covered all the courts.'
segments.s614-g12: به پای آورد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: به + پای + آورد literally 'bring to the foot', i.e. 'humble/bring low'. کاخ و ایوانها are the coordinated direct objects 'palaces and halls'. The verb آورد is past/present-stem here meaning 'brought'.
segments.s614-g13: ز ... ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' repeated for emphasis ('from king and from dervish'). هر کو is an archaic relative expression meaning 'whoever' (هر = 'every/any', کو = relative 'who'); بمرد is an optative/subjunctive form 'dies'/'may die' used generically. The clause sets up a universal statement about anyone who dies.
segments.s614-g14: ابا here is the verb 'to be reluctant/refuse' used adverbially (cf. poetic usages) and combines with خویشتن 'with himself' to mark accompanying carriage; نام نیکی ('a good name/reputation') is the thing carried, and ببرد is a present-subjunctive/optative third-person singular of بردن 'to carry/take'. The clause thus says that on dying one takes a good reputation with oneself.
segments.s614-g15: ز introduces the source/realm 'of the world' (گیتی). ستایش is the subject/noun 'praise' and به مابر is the prepositional phrase 'to/for us' (به + ما + بر treated as a fused poetic form). بس است is the predicate 'is enough/sufficient'. The clause states that worldly praise directed at us suffices.
segments.s614-g16: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause. گنج درم ('treasure of coin/wealth') is the subject, بهر is the preposition 'for/to', and دیگر کس 'other person/another' is the beneficiary; است is the copula 'is'. Grammatically: 'that treasure/wealth in coin is for someone else.'
segments.s614-g129: به ... آمد is a motion-to construction: به + place (پرده‌سرا) + آمد (simple past) = 'came to the pavilion.' از بیشه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's grove' (بیشه modified by شاه), placed before the verb as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s614-g130: This segment lists the people who arrived. ابا here functions as a proper name (appositive) followed by titles/roles: موبد 'mōbed/priest' and پهلوان سپاه 'the army's champion' (پهلوان modified by سپاه). The coordinating و links موبد and پهلوان سپاه; given the context, ابا is best read as a name, not the lexical sense 'to refuse'.
segments.s614-g131: همی + خواند is the archaic continuous past: 'kept on singing/shouting.' لشکر is the agent (the army) and برو آفرین are the acclamatory words being uttered; برو is an imperative 'go' and آفرین is an acclamation ('bravo').
segments.s614-g132: که introduces the content/clause of the shout. بی‌تو 'without you' is the prepositional phrase; مبادا is the negative jussive 'may it not be' (a wish/curse form). کلاه و نگین are the things wished not to exist 'without you' (kinds of headgear/jewel).
segments.s614-g133: به + place (خرگاه) + شد uses شد as the past tense marker meaning 'arrived/was made'—here 'went/arrived to the stable.' چون + clause (چون سپه بازگشت) is temporal 'when/as the army returned.' سپه is the subject of بازگشت ('returned').
segments.s614-g134: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'because of' or 'from.' دادنش is the nouned/possessive form of دادن ('his giving' or 'the act of his giving'); it governs the result clause. گیتی 'the world' is the subject of the predicate پرآواز گشت, where پرآواز is a compound adjective 'full of sound/acclaim' and گشت is 'became.'
segments.s614-g135: یکی دانشی is the indefinite 'a certain/one' + adjective-like noun 'learned/knowledgeable' modifying مرزبان (a border-governor). پیش‌کار follows as an appositive/title 'an attendant' or 'one in charge' — together they describe a specific official.
segments.s614-g136: به marks motion toward the place: 'to the (خرگاه) stable.' نو modifies خرگاه ('new stable'). بر here has the locative/positional sense 'on/over' and پراگنده خار is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over scattered thorns' (پراگنده qualifying خار).
segments.s614-g137: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن/نهاد (to put/place) meaning “they placed.” It is followed by a simple coordinated list of direct objects (کافور و مشک و گلاب = camphor, musk, and rosewater). The verb-first order is normal in poetic narrative here.
segments.s614-g138: بگسترد is a narrative/past form meaning “(he/they) spread.” It takes مشک as its object. از بر is a classical locative combination with بر (“upon/on”) so از + بر جای خواب means “upon the place of sleep” (i.e. upon the bed/bedchamber).
segments.s614-g139: همه خیمه‌ها is a locative/fronted phrase “in/throughout all the tents.” خوان زرین is the noun phrase “a golden spread/banquet.” نهاد here is the past of نهادن (placed/set), 3rd-person (poetic variation between plural and singular subjects appears in the narrative).
segments.s614-g140: برو functions here as a discourse particle/connector (thereupon/then) in the narrative; it precedes the noun phrase. کاسه آرایش چین is a compound noun phrase: کاسه (a bowl/goblet) modified by آرایش (adornment/ornament) and چین (here “China,” i.e. of Chinese make or style). نهاد again is ‘placed/put.’
segments.s614-g141: بیاراست is a past tense verb meaning “he/she adorned/arrayed.” سالار خوان means “the master/manager of the table” (سالار = chief/master, خوان = banquet/table). از بره uses از to mark the material/ingredient (“with/from lamb”), so the steward prepared the feast using lamb.
segments.s614-g142: همه خوردنیها = “all the edibles/foods.” که is a relative pronoun “which/that.” بد یکسره in the printed text functions idiomatically as classical/poetic phrasing meaning roughly “altogether/in one go” (بد here is an orthographic/poetic form closely related to به, and یکسره means “completely/at once”), so the clause qualifies how the foods were presented or served.
segments.s614-g143: چو … شد is a temporal clause “when … had happened.” چو = “when,” نان خورده شد = “the bread was eaten” (خورده = past participle ‘eaten,’ شد as auxiliary/past). شاه بهرام گور is the subject phrase: the king Bahram Gūr (گور here is the epithet ‘Gūr/onager’ attached to Bahram).
segments.s614-g144: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-verb meaning “he commanded/ordered.” جامی بزرگ از بلور is the object phrase: جامی (a goblet/cup), بزرگ (large), از بلور (made of crystal). Word order places the verb before its object in this narrative sentence.
segments.s614-g105: This is an introductory clause: 'یکی گفت' (someone said) + a vocative phrase 'کای شاه خورشید چهر' addressing the king. 'کای' is the archaic vocative 'O'; 'خورشید چهر' is a poetic epithet 'sun-faced' (noun + noun used attributively). Word order is subject-verb ('یکی گفت') then the vocative/title phrase.
segments.s614-g106: This is the main clause asking about pity/affection: the verb 'نداری' is the negative 2nd-person present ('you do not have') in archaic surface form; 'همی' is an old emphatic/continuous particle ('still/always' or continuous aspect) placed before the predicate; 'بر تن خویش' = 'on your body' with 'بر' (on/upon) + 'تن خویش' ('one's own body' split here as 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش'); 'مهر' is the noun 'affection/pity' and is the object/thing lacking. Overall: 'Do you not have affection upon your own body?'
segments.s614-g107: Subject + predicate + accompaniment: 'همه بیشه شیرند' literally 'the whole thicket are lions' (predicate 'شیرند' = 'are lions' follows the subject), then 'با بچگان' ('with their young') adds accompaniment. The plural copular/verb form '-ند' marks the predicate.
segments.s614-g108: Elliptical/categorical statement: 'همه بچگان' (all the cubs) followed by a locative/genitive phrase 'شیر مادر مکان' — here 'شیر مادر' = 'mother-lion' (noun + genitive) and 'مکان' marks 'place/den'. The copula is omitted; the phrase functions as 'all the cubs are in the mother-lion's place/den.'
segments.s614-g109: 'کنون باید' = 'now it is necessary' (bâyad + infinitive pattern); here the infinitival idea is expressed by the noun-like phrase 'آژیر بودن' ('to be/sound an alarm') where 'بودن' is the infinitive/nominalizing 'to be'. Word order places the obligation first and the action after, with 'دلیر' (the brave) as the understood agent: 'the brave must be sounding an alarm.'
segments.s614-g110: 'که' introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'); 'در مهرگان' is a locative phrase ('at/among Mehregan' — a place or festival); the small clause 'بچه دارد به زیر' uses 'دارد' (has) followed by the locative phrase 'به زیر' ('underneath, beneath') so the order is verb then its locative complement: 'a mother has a young beneath her.' This is a concise, slightly archaic construction for 'there is a young under (her)'.
segments.s614-g111: A locative prepositional phrase giving distance: 'سه فرسنگ بالای این بیشه است' = 'three parasangs above this thicket is (it)'. Numeral + measure word precede the locative 'بالای این بیشه', and the copula 'است' closes the clause. This places a location at a distance from the previously mentioned grove.
segments.s614-g112: This is a conditional temporal phrase: 'به یک سال اگر' = 'if in one year / within a year' (archaic ordering places the time expression before 'اگر'). 'شیرگیری' is a deverbal noun 'milking / taking to milk' (here contextually 'to obtain/capture a cub for raising'), and 'به دست' ('by hand') is an instrument phrase placed after it. The verb of the condition is omitted here (ellipsis continues in the next lines), so the segment reads like 'if, within a year, you get a cub by hand...'.
segments.s614-g49: که here introduces an explanatory/casual subordinate clause (’for/since’). آن جای گرزست is a copular clause with the archaic fused copula -ست attached to گرز (گرز+است → گرزست), meaning ‘that place is of the mace/for maces.’ The final و تیر و کمان simply adds 'and arrows and bow' as coordinated objects of the place description.
segments.s614-g50: نباشیم is a first‑person plural negative subjunctive/optative form (ن‑ + باشیم) meaning essentially ‘we should not be / we must not remain.’ بی‌تاختن is an adverbial gerundial phrase ‘without charging/attacking’ (بی‑ + verb‑stem). یک زمان is a temporal phrase ‘a single moment,’ so the whole clause means ‘we must not remain without charging even a moment.’
segments.s614-g51: بیابان که من دیده‌ام is a relative clause 'the desert that I have seen' (که as relative 'that/which'). زیر جز is a compact, poetic phrasing: جز here means 'nothing but/except' and زیر (literally 'under') combines idiomatically with شده in the next segment to express 'has become nothing but.'
segments.s614-g52: شده is the perfect/passive 'has become.' چون introduces the simile 'like.' بن (base/root) modifies نیزه (spear) so that چون بن نیزه بالای گز reads 'like the bases of spears above the tamarisk'; بالای is the preposition 'above/on top of' and گز is the tamarisk tree (a landscape image).
segments.s614-g53: بران جایگه نیز is a locative phrase 'on/at that place as well' (بران = 'on/thereupon'). یابیم is a classical present/subjunctive form meaning 'we find/might find' (word order here places شیر 'lion' after the verb for poetic inversion: object at the end).
segments.s614-g54: شکاری here qualifies شیر in the previous segment and means 'hunting/predatory' (a hunting lion). بودگر is the archaic/poetic conditional particle (بود + گر → 'if it were'). بمانیم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we would remain' or 'we would be left,' and دیر is an adverb 'long' (of time). Together: 'if it were (a hunting one), we would remain long.'
segments.s614-g55: همی بود is a running/commentative past 'it continued to be so' (همی = still/continually + بود = was). تا introduces the temporal limit 'until.' ابر شهریوری is a metaphorical compound 'a cloud of Shahrivar' (شهریوری = 'of Shahrivar' — here likely a proper-name epithet for a host).
segments.s614-g56: برآمد is 'arose/appeared.' جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد as resultative/copular). پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر = full, از = of), and لشکری is 'troops/ soldiery'; altogether 'the world became full of troops.'
segments.s614-g177: بدان is be- + dān (“know”) used as an imperative/announcement meaning “know that…”. کس is the indirect object “that person/those people.” دهم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of دادن used here as “I give” or “I grant.” چیز او را is the definite direct object: چیز (thing) + او (his) + را (object marker); که introduces the relative clause “which/that …”.
segments.s614-g178: ازو is the prepositional pronoun “from/of him.” بستد (بَستَد) is an older/poetic perfect/past-form of بَستن used here in the sense “shut off/withheld (from him).” رنج او دید is an idiomatic light-verb construction: رنج + دید(ن) = “to suffer” or “to experience hardship”; و ... نیز = “and ... also.”
segments.s614-g179: وگر is a conditional particle “if/ but if.” اسپ is the subject “steed/horse.” در کشت‌زاری is a locative phrase “in the cultivated field/farmland” (در + کشت‌زار). کند is the 3rd-person singular present of کردن/کردن here meaning “does/works” (i.e. “labors/works”).
segments.s614-g180: ور (variant of وگر) introduces an alternative conditional “or if.” آهنگ here means “intention/aim” or “inclination,” and بر is the preposition “toward/on/for.” میوه‌داری is a deverbal/noun phrase “fruit-bearing/fruitfulness.” کند is again “does/is so.” The clause means “or if it is intended/aimed at producing fruit.”
segments.s614-g181: ز (poetic form of از) = “from.” زندان = “prison.” نیابد is the classical negative present of یابیدن (niyābad) = “does not find/obtain,” used here to mean “will not obtain” (i.e. “will not be granted/found”). به سالی is a temporal/durational phrase “for a year/within a year.” رها = “release/free.” Together the clause says that release from prison will not occur within a year.
segments.s614-g182: سوار is the noun “rider/horseman” (here the subject described). سرافراز‌گر is a compound participial/agentive adjective meaning “exalting/pride‑making” or simply “proud/glorious” as an epithet. بی‌بها is an adjective “without price/priceless (invaluable).” The phrase is a nominal description attached to the person who will not be freed.
segments.s614-g183: همان = “that/same.” رنج = “hardship/suffering.” ما = “we.” بس is an adverb “enough” or “sufficiently.” گزیدست is an archaic/perfect form (from گزیدن) here meaning roughly “have tasted/experienced” or “have endured/selected” (classically used for having undergone something). بهر = “for/for the sake of/so that.” The clause expresses that we have suffered that hardship sufficiently.
segments.s614-g184: بیاییم is the hortative/optative 1st-person-plural form of آمدن: “let us come/let us go.” و = “and.” آزرده = “distressed/afflicted” (adjective/predicative). گردند (from گشتن/گردیدن) here is a present/subjunctive form “become” (they/it become), and شهر = “the city.” The clause says “let us come, and the city will become distressed.”
segments.s614-g89: 'ولیکن' is a conjunctive adverb meaning 'but'. The phrase 'به شمشیر یازم به شیر' puts the instrument ('به شمشیر' — literally 'by/with the sword') before the verb 'یازم' (an archaic first‑person present/future form meaning 'I burst forth/assail'), followed by the target 'به شیر' ('at/to the lion'); word order is Instrument — Verb(1sg) — Goal.
segments.s614-g90: 'بدان' here is the prefixed form (ب + دان) introducing the result/purpose clause ('so that he/they may know' / 'so that it be known'). 'تا' explicitly marks the purpose/result 'so that'. 'نخواند' is negative present/subjunctive/optative meaning 'not call' (here 'not call [me]'). 'م' + 'را' are the enclitic object pronoun + object marker forming 'مرا' ('me' as object). 'نادلیر' is the predicate nominal 'coward' (an older form: na-dilir). Together: 'so that they will not call me a coward.'
segments.s614-g91: 'بپوشید' is a past tense (3rd person) verb meaning 'he put on/he clothed himself in'. The modifiers 'ترکرده' (a past participle 'moistened/dampened') and 'پشمین' ('woolen') precede the head noun 'قبای' ('robe'), forming the noun phrase 'the (recently) dampened woolen robe' which is the object of 'بپوشید'.
segments.s614-g92: The clause 'به اسپ نبرد اندر آورد پای' describes the mounting action. 'به اسپ' is a prepositional phrase 'onto/onto the horse'; 'نبرد' here is an adverbial/nominal sense 'for battle' (classical usage) or glossing the action context; 'اندر آورد' is a compound verb 'he brought/put in' (literally 'brought into'), and 'پای' is the direct object 'the foot' — together 'he put his foot into (the stirrup) as he mounted the horse.'
segments.s614-g93: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The sequence 'شیر اژدها دید' follows classical Persian word order where the subject 'شیر' (lion) precedes the object 'اژدها' (dragon) and the verb 'دید' (saw) closes the clause. 'بر پای خاست' is a light‑verb clause 'خاست' (rose) with the prepositional phrase 'بر پای' ('to his feet/on his feet') indicating the manner of rising.
segments.s614-g94: 'ز بالا' ('from above') gives the direction/source. 'دو دست' is the direct object 'both hands'. 'اندر آورد' (past) is 'he brought/raised in/into' and 'راست' here is an adverbial directional/qualifying word 'to the right' or 'straight' — so the clause means 'he raised both hands upward and to the right.' The subject is continued from the previous clause (he: the lion).
segments.s614-g95: 'همی' is a modal/adverbial particle often marking immediacy ('was just about to', 'was on the point of'). 'خواست' (past) is 'he intended/wanted [to]'; 'زد' is the infinitive/lexical verb 'to strike'. The prepositional phrase 'بر سر اسپ اوی' places the intended blow 'upon the head/top of his horse' with 'اوی' an archaic enclitic pronoun 'his/it/him' referring back to the steed or rider; the whole segment describes an intended strike directed at the horse's head/top.
segments.s614-g96: 'بزد' is the simple past 'he struck'. 'پاشنه' ('heel') is the direct object; 'مرد' ('man') names whose heel is struck or who is involved; 'نخچیر جوی' is a compound description 'nakhchīr‑jū' meaning 'prey‑seeking' or 'hunter/one seeking booty' (an adjectival phrase modifying 'مرد'). Reading together: 'he struck the man's heel — the prey‑seeking man' (i.e. the man/hunter who sought the spoil was struck/reacted).
segments.s614-g161: مبادا is an optative/negative wish meaning “may it not be” or “let there not be.” جز introduces an exception: “except.” از marks the source/partitive (“of/from”), followed by نیکویی “goodness/virtue.” در جهان is a locative phrase “in the world.” Word order is poetic: the exception phrase (جز از نیکویی) appears before the locative.
segments.s614-g162: ز من uses the short/poetic preposition ز (= از) plus من as object “from me.” در میان is the compound locative “in the midst / among.” کهان و مهان are two coordinated nouns describing groups among whom the speaker speaks; they are linked to جز previously, so the whole unit means “among the X and the Y.” Here من is object (“me”), not the subject.
segments.s614-g163: بیارید is an imperative “bring (him/it)” appearing before گفتا because the quoted command is placed ahead of the reporting verb; here you should read it as the content of what is said. گفتا is the classical past/simple narrative “said.” منادیگری is an agent-noun derived from منادی “herald” (the office or act of heralding) and identifies the speaker.
segments.s614-g164: خوش‌آواز is an adjectival phrase “melodious (of voice)” modifying the herald. و links this descriptor to another appositive: از نامداران سری literally “one from the renowned, a chief/head,” i.e. “a notable man among the famed.” از before نامداران marks selection (“one of/from the renowned”), and سری here means “head/chief” or a person of rank.
segments.s614-g165: که introduces a relative/result clause (“so that/which”). گردد is the classical third‑person verb form meaning “become / turn / go” (here “may go/become”), used in a poetic narrative. سراسر is an adverb “throughout/entire,” and به گرد سپاه is a prepositional phrase “around the host/army.” Together: “which will go all around the army.”
segments.s614-g166: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning “ever/still/continually.” برخروشد is a classical intransitive verb meaning “to encounter / meet / come upon” (here “will be met/encountered”). به بی‌راه و راه is a binary phrase “on the off‑road (wrong/unwarranted) and the road (right/path),” i.e. “both off the road and on it.” The two nouns are parallel and give the sense of encountering people everywhere.
segments.s614-g167: بگوید is the narrative verb “he says / will say” (poetic present/future usage). که introduces the content clause (“that …”). بر کوی بر شهر stacks two locative prepositional phrases (بر = “on/upon”) for emphasis: “on the street, in the city.” جز begins the exception clause “except …” which will be completed by the following segment.
segments.s614-g168: گر here introduces the conditional/exclusive sense “if / unless (it be).” از marks material or source “of/from,” followed by a list of valuables — گوهر (gem), زر (gold), دیبا (brocade), خز (fur/velvet). The phrase completes the exception begun by جز: “except if it be of jewels, gold, brocade, or fur.”
segments.s614-g169: چنین تا functions as a result/purpose conjunction 'thus/so that'. به + noun introduces the target 'onto' (to/into) and is followed by the noun خاشاک 'chaff'; the adjectives ناچیز (insignificant) and پست (low/base) follow the noun in postnominal position, a common poetic order.
segments.s614-g170: بیازد is a third-person verb form meaning 'casts/throws' (classical narrative present); it follows the purpose-marking تا. کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone', and ناسزاوار modifies دست ('an unworthy/insult‑deserving hand'); دست here is used metonymically for the agent/power doing the casting. Verb-before-subject word order is acceptable in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s614-g171: بر اسپش 'on/over his steed' uses بر + اسپ + enclitic ش ('his'). نشانم is نشان + ـم: here نشـان is a noun 'mark/sign' with the enclitic ـم (possessive 'my'), i.e. 'my mark' (the annotation lists ـم oddly as #هست but here it is possessive). ز پس کرده روی is a participial/clausal sequence: ز (from) + پس (back/behind) + کرده (perfective participle 'having made/done') + روی (face), together meaning 'having turned his face away/back' (i.e. 'he turned his face from behind').
segments.s614-g172: ز ایدر کشان is a participial phrase: ز ایدر 'from here' and کشان (present participle 'dragging, pulling')—'dragging (him) from here'. با introduces a comitative phrase 'with two پرخاشجوی', where پرخاشجوی is used as a noun 'quarrelsome/aggressive ones'. This participle attaches to the preceding action and describes how they move him.
segments.s614-g173: دو پایش is دو پا 'two feet' + enclitic ش 'his' (his two feet). ببندند is third-person plural present/jussive of بستن 'to bind/tie' (they bind/tie). در زیر اسپ is a double locative 'in/under the steed' (در + زیر + اسپ), a classical poetic locative emphasizing 'under the horse'.
segments.s614-g174: فرستمش is فرستم (I send) with the enclitic ـش 'him' = 'I send him'. تا here introduces the destination/purpose ('to' / 'to the place of') and is followed by the title + name خان آذرگشسپ ('Khan Azargashasp'), the recipient. In context this is a purpose/destination clause: 'I send him to Khan Azargashasp.'
segments.s614-g175: نیایش is the noun 'supplication/prayer'. کند is the subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form of کردن 'to do' used here with the noun to form the verbal idea 'may he pray' (subordinate purpose after فرستمش تا). پیش آتش 'before the fire' means 'in the presence of the fire'; به خاک 'to the ground' indicates prostration or touching the earth as part of the devotion.
segments.s614-g176: پرستش 'worship' with کند again forms 'may he worship' (subjunctive). پیش یزدان 'before Yezdan' means 'in the presence of God/divinity', and پاک is an epithet 'pure' modifying یزدان. Word order places the adjective after the noun, as common in Persian poetic register.
segments.s614-g97: بزد is the simple past of زدن (3rd sing.), 'he struck/struck at'. The prepositional phrase بر سر شیر marks the target ('on/at the lion's head'), and شمشیر تیز is the instrument phrase 'sharp sword' with the adjective تیز following the noun (typical Persian adjective order).
segments.s614-g98: سبک modifies جفت here, yielding 'the light/nimble mate'; او is the third‑person possessive referring back to the lion. جست is the past of 'to leap/spring' and راه گریز is a compound 'path/way of escape' (راه + گریز). The phrase order is poetically compact but reads: 'its nimble mate leapt to the path of flight.'
segments.s614-g99: ز ... تا ... (ز سر تا میانش) gives the extent 'from the head to his middle' (میانش = 'his middle' with enclitic -ش). بدونیم کرد uses بدون (literally 'without') with کرد ('made/caused') to indicate he caused the creature to be bereft/deprived over that extent — a terse, poetic way to say he rendered him maimed/stripped from head to midriff.
segments.s614-g100: دل نره شیران is a genitival description 'the hearts of the male lions' (نره qualifying شیران). پر از بیم کرد is a causative construction: پر از 'full of' + بیم 'fear', and کرد 'he made/caused' — 'he made their hearts full of fear.'
segments.s614-g101: بیامد is the past 'came'. دگر modifies شیر as 'another' (an additional/other lion). The adjectives غران (roaring) and دلیر (brave) follow شیر in typical Persian adjective position: 'another roaring, brave lion came.'
segments.s614-g102: همی is a durative/continuous adverb 'still/continually'. جفت او = 'his mate/partner' (جفت = mate, او = his). بچه پرورد is a compact verbal phrase 'reared/was rearing the young' (پرورد is the past of پروردن 'to rear'), and زیر 'beneath/under' gives the location. Word order is compressed/poetic: 'his mate beneath kept rearing the young.'
segments.s614-g103: بزد is past 'he struck'. خنجری تیز is 'a sharp dagger' (noun + adjective). بر گردنش is the prepositional target 'at/on his neck' with the possessive enclitic -ش on گردن. The clause expresses the strike made against the neck.
segments.s614-g104: سر شیر نر = 'the head of the male lion' (noun sequence: head + lion + 'male'). کنده شد is a passive construction: کنده (past participle of کندن 'to tear/untie') + شد (auxiliary 'was'), hence 'was torn off'. از تنش 'from his body' uses از 'from' plus the possessive -ش. The whole: 'the male lion's head was torn from his body.'
segments.s614-g145: 'که' is a relative pronoun here ('who/that') introducing the clause. 'آرد' is the 3rd‑person singular literary form of آوردن 'to bring' (simple/past narrative in epic style), and its object is the compound noun 'پری‌چهره میگسار' = 'fair‑faced cupbearer' where the adjective precedes the noun.
segments.s614-g146: 'نهد' is the 3rd‑person singular of نهادن 'to place/set' (the main verb of this short clause). 'بر' + 'کف' is a prepositional phrase 'on the palm'. 'دادگر شهریار' is a noun phrase where 'دادگر' qualifies 'شهریار' = 'the just king'; the implied object ('her') is carried over from the previous segment.
segments.s614-g147: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said/spoke' (a formulaic narrative phrase); 'کان' is an archaic demonstrative 'that' (prefacing a title), followed by the title and name 'شهریار اردشیر' ('king Ardashir'). Word order places the demonstrative before the title.
segments.s614-g148: The clause introduced by 'که' reports what he said ('that/that which'). 'برنا شد' = 'became youthful' (برنا = youthful, شد = became). 'از بخت او' is a prepositional/source phrase 'by/from his fortune' (از marking cause/source). 'مرد پیر' is the nominal subject 'an old man' (in this literary order the predicate 'برنا شد' appears before the noun phrase it describes).
segments.s614-g149: 'سر مایه او بود' = 'was his principal resource/capital' (سر + مایه forming a compound noun, followed by the possessor 'او' and the past copula 'بود'). 'ما کهتریم' is a linked statement 'we are the lesser/lowly ones' (ما subject + کهتریم, first‑person plural predicate).
segments.s614-g150: This is a prototypical conditional introduced by 'اگر' ('if'). 'کهتری را' marks 'the inferior/lowlier (one)' as the definite object (را). 'خود' emphasizes the actor ('ourselves'), 'اندر' = 'in/within' (poetic/inherent locative), and 'خوریم' is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive of خوردن 'to eat' ('we eat/should eat'). The clause literally reads 'if we ourselves eat the lowlier.'
segments.s614-g151: A parallel series of prepositional phrases introduced by repeated 'به' marking domains/occasions: 'به رزم' (in battle), 'به بزم' (at the feast), 'به رای' (in counsel/advice), 'به خوان' (at the table/feast). The repeated 'به' is a poetic device listing the spheres in which the following injunction applies.
segments.s614-g152: 'جز او را' = 'except him' (جز = except; را marks the following as the focused object). 'جهاندار گیتی' is a compounded title 'world‑ruler / sovereign of the world'. 'مخوان' here is a negative imperative form ('do not call/declare'); Persian often places the object before the verb, so the verb at the end gives the command 'do not call anyone the world's sovereign except him.'
segments.s614-g1: "بفرمود تا" is a classical polite/past reporting verb + subordinator: بفرمود (honorific past 'he ordered/said') governs a تا‑clause expressing the commanded action ('that ...'). "تخت شاهنشهی" is a noun phrase ('the imperial throne') with شاهنشهی qualifying تخت; word order is verb + تا‑clause (common in older Persian: 'he commanded that ...').
segments.s614-g2: This is the content of the تا‑clause. "به باغ بهار" = 'into/to the spring‑garden' (به + place), "اندر آرد" uses آرد (classical form of آوردن) in a present/subjunctive/reportive sense after تا to express the commanded bringing ('be brought'/'bring'), and "رهی" is a short archaic/poetic adverbial (literally 'on the way' / 'thereupon') placed at the end; overall: 'that (they) bring (it) into the spring garden.'
segments.s614-g3: "به فرمان" is a prepositional phrase 'by command' (به used adverbially = 'by/under'), followed by ببردند, a poetic past form meaning 'they carried/took' (3rd person plural). "پیروزه تخت" is an inverted adjective + noun ('turquoise throne'); poetry often inverts normal Persian order ('تخت پیروزه').
segments.s614-g4: نهادند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/set' ('they placed'). "زیر گلفشان درخت" gives the location and the object: زیر گلفشان = 'under the flower‑sprayer/flowering canopy' (prepositional phrase) and درخت = 'a tree' (object). Poetic word order places the locative phrase before the noun object.
segments.s614-g5: می‌و جام بردند is a coordinated noun phrase + verb: می (here 'wine/fruit' in poetic register) and جام ('cup/goblet') with بردند (past 3rd pl. 'they carried/went with'). The conjunction و links to the following noun رامشگران ('the entertainers/comforters'), so the clause lists what was carried and who accompanied.
segments.s614-g6: "به پالیز رفتند" is past 3rd‑person plural 'they went into the orchard' (به + place + رفتند). "با مهتران" is an accompaniment phrase 'with the chiefs/nobles' (با + plural). In older poetry مهتران = 'the great/leading men'; word order is straightforward: verb then adjuncts.
segments.s614-g7: چنین گفت is a conventional formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' in classical narrative). "با رای‌زن شهریار" indicates the speaker or addressee relationship: با + رای‌زن + شهریار can be read as 'with the king's counselor' or 'the royal counselor said' depending on context; grammatically با introduces the person involved (either accompaniment or the person addressed).
segments.s614-g8: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. "خرم" is a predicate adjective 'joyful/pleasant', "به مردم" is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for the people', "بود" is the past copula 'was', and "روزگار" ('the times') appears as the subject; the clause therefore says 'that the times were joyful for the people.' Poetic word order places the adjective/predicate before the subject.
segments.s614-g121: سواران گردنکش اندر زمان is a noun phrase with an archaic/pre-classical order: سواران (plural 'horsemen') followed by the adjective گردنکش ('headstrong, rebellious') and the locative phrase اندر زمان ('in/among the time/age'). اندر is an older preposition meaning 'in' or 'among'.
segments.s614-g122: نکردند is the past negative, third-person plural ('they did not do/make'). نامی here functions as 'a name' / 'renown' (derived from the adjective but used as the object). به is used in the instrumental/agentive sense 'by/with' and تیر و کمان = 'bow and arrow' (weapons). The whole clause means 'they did not make a name by bow and arrow.'
segments.s614-g123: This conditional begins with اگر ('if'). داد مردی is a genitive or compound idea 'the justice/manliness of a man' (داد used as noun 'justice' or 'right'; مردی emphasizes manhood/valor). بخواهیم is present-subjunctive/indicative 'we desire/ask (for)'. The final داد repeats the noun 'justice' (the object sought) and echoes the first داد for emphasis.
segments.s614-g124: به introduces the means ('by/with'). گوپال here is treated as a proper name (Gūpāl) rather than the literal 'cowherd' in this context; و links 'and' شمشیر 'sword'. گیریم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/imperative-ish 'we take/seize' or 'we will take', and یاد is a noun 'remembrance'/'memorial' — so 'we will take it by Gūpāl and sword as a remembrance.'
segments.s614-g125: بدو is an enclitic/old-fashioned prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e., 'he said to him'). گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (the supplied sense list does not include 'said', so the exact sense index is not available). موبد is the subject 'the mobed (Zoroastrian priest)'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. مرد سوار is a fixed compound meaning 'a valiant/heroic rider' (mard used in the sense 'hero').
segments.s614-g126: نبیند is a negative present/past form from دیدن used impersonally here to mean 'is not seen' or 'one does not see.' چو = 'like/as'; تو = 'you' (poetic thou); گرد in this context functions idiomatically in the clause 'چو تو گرد' to mark likeness/equivalence (older usage, glossed here as 'around/about' but functioning as part of the phrase 'one like you'); در کارزار = 'in battle/ on the battlefield.'
segments.s614-g127: که introduces a wish clause. چشم بد is the idiom 'evil eye' (literally 'bad eye'). از marks origin/source 'from'; فر is the special poetic noun 'farr' meaning royal glory or divine radiance; تو = 'your'. دور is the adjective 'far/distant' and باد is the optative particle 'may' (a conventional wish marker in classical Persian).
segments.s614-g128: نشست here means 'seat/assembly/sitting' (a noun: session or place to sit). تو = 'your'; در گلشن = 'in the garden' (گلشن = garden, a poetic place of delight). و سور = 'and a feast/banquet'; باد again is the optative 'may' — so the clause is a wish: 'May your seat be in the garden and a feast.'

sentences.s614-s57: The world will not become empty of brave men; why, then, do you heap so much suffering upon your body?

sentences.s614-s78: How dishonourable and brutal he was — when he slew thirty-six of the kings.

sentences.s614-s34: They arranged so as to set covers over the pits; on those covers they made (or placed) crimson Chinese cloths.
sentences.s614-s35: Then the army charged in together; the king himself and his nobles rode on to the hunting‑ground.

sentences.s614-s23: Now the onager’s neck becomes stout; the male lion’s heart takes courage, and the tiger takes on its hue.
sentences.s614-s24: One must not take hounds and cheetahs, with lamp and falcon and hawk, on a long road.
sentences.s614-s29: From every quarter a troop of warriors turned their faces toward the King of Iran.
sentences.s614-s30: From among them he chose the proud-necked (rebels) — anyone who bore a mark from the spoil.

sentences.s614-s32: They carried off the stable and the curtained pavilion — that same tent, its appendages, and the beasts of burden.
sentences.s614-s13: When camphor came, the musk became spoiled; by the camphor the crown turned unbecoming.
sentences.s614-s14: I will continue feasting and playing for two years; when a short while is ended, it will come to the mane.
sentences.s614-s15: I will go before God and clothe myself in a coarse cloak; may I not be ungrateful because of his words.
sentences.s614-s16: I passed many days in joy; from a wind I took an ill share.
sentences.s614-s41: When this thicket is emptied of lions, my dagger will become my grave on the road.
sentences.s614-s42: That night and at early dawn the king and his army set out toward the thicket.

sentences.s614-s44: Bahram Gerd thus said to his companions: 'I have bow and arrows, and a hand that wins.'
sentences.s614-s9: Be harmless and upright, if you want that this which has been eaten will not harm you.
sentences.s614-s10: Now my year has gone to thirty‑eight; many days of rejoicing have passed.
sentences.s614-s11: When the youthful year draws on to forty, the sorrow of the day of death enters the heart.
sentences.s614-s12: When a single hair becomes white on the head, one must break (cast off) hope for joy.
sentences.s614-s17: Now I have no golden goblet of wine for the rose, the flame, the apple, and the quince; it is empty.
sentences.s614-s18: When I see the apple's face with its variegated color, the sky becomes like the back of a leopard.

sentences.s614-s40: First, with the sword we will fell the lion; likewise we will fell that brave dragon.

sentences.s614-s65: From the king's grove to the pavilion came Aba, the mōbed, and the army's champion.
